chunks.text: James O'Brien is a Professor of Computer Science at UC Berkeley. His research interests include computer graphics, computer animation, simulations of physical systems, human perception, rendering, image synthesis, machine learning, and the forensic analysis of images and video. He has worked with film and game companies on integrating advanced simulation physics into games and special effects, with his destruction modeling methods used in over 200 feature films and game titles.
chunks.question: What are James O'Brien's main research areas?
chunks.answer: Computer graphics, animation, and image forensics.

chunks.text: Prof. O'Brien was Chief Scientist at Pixelux and Avametric, and is currently a technical advisor to Juice Labs and Chief Scientist/co-founder at Get Klothed. He has served as an expert witness or consulting expert in multiple patent cases. In 2015, he received an Academy Award for Technical Achievement for his work in destruction modeling. He earned his Ph.D. from Georgia Institute of Technology in 2000, where his thesis received the Outstanding Doctoral Dissertation Award.
chunks.question: What award did James O'Brien receive in 2015?

chunks.text: Additional honors include being a Sloan Fellow, recipient of the Jim and Donna Gray Endowment Award for Excellence in Undergraduate Teaching, and FIU College of Engineering Distinguished Alumni. He was selected for Technology Review's TR-35, awarded Georgia Tech's 15 Year Impact award, and received research fellowships from the Okawa and Hellman Foundations. From 2010 through 2016, he served as Director at Large for ACM SIGGRAPH. In 2020, he received a Technical Achievement award from Dreamworks Animation.
chunks.question: What role did James O'Brien hold at ACM SIGGRAPH?

chunks.text: Education: 2000, Ph.D., Computer Science, Georgia Institute of Technology; 1997, M.S., Computer Science, Georgia Institute of Technology; 1992, B.S., Computer Science, Florida International University. Research areas include Graphics (GR), Artificial Intelligence (AI), Human-Computer Interaction (HCI), and Scientific Computing (SCI), with specific interests in computer animation, physical simulation, surface modeling, human perception of motion, computational geometry, image processing, and scientific computing.
chunks.question: Where did James O'Brien earn his Ph.D.?

chunks.text: Research centers associated with Prof. O'Brien include Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research Lab (BAIR), Berkeley Center for New Media (BCNM), Center for Information Technology Research in the Interest of Society - The Banatao Institute (CITRIS), Computer Graphics at Berkeley (B-CAM), FHL Vive Center for Enhanced Reality, and Visual Computing Lab (VCL). Teaching schedule includes CS 184 and CS 284A, both Foundations of Computer Graphics courses held TuTh 14:00-15:29.
chunks.question: Which courses does James O'Brien teach?
chunks.answer: CS 184 and CS 284A, Foundations of Computer Graphics.

chunks.text: Selected publications focus on topics such as image forensic labeling, facial deformations, fake image credibility, deformation approximations, svBRDF estimation from video, and simulation of joint and fault networks. Notable journals include Harvard Kennedy School Misinformation Review, ACM Transactions on Graphics, New Media and Society, and IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics, with conferences like Eurographics Symposium on Rendering and ACM SIGGRAPH.
chunks.question: What is a key topic in James O'Brien's publications?

chunks.text: Selected honors and awards include Technical Achievement (Oscar) Award in 2015, ACM Distinguished Scientist Award in 2009, Jim and Donna Gray Award for Excellence in Undergraduate Teaching of Computer Science in 2008, Hellman Fellow in 2004, MIT Tech Review Top 100 Innovators Under 35 (TR100) in 2004, Sloan Research Fellow in 2003, and Okawa Research Grant in 2000. These recognitions highlight contributions to computer graphics, teaching, and innovation.
chunks.question: When did James O'Brien receive the ACM Distinguished Scientist Award?
